Event: As a child, he wants to steal his cousin's jump rope
Explanation: Desire to take cousin’s property would benefit him while depriving cousin.

Event: He spitefully ties his cousin's jump rope to a truck in envy
Explanation: He destroys the jump rope out of spite, giving himself no benefit and harming his cousin.

Event: He frequently tries to impress many girls but rarely succeeds
Explanation: His repeated failed attempts cost him time/effort with little gain while causing mild social/attention costs to others.

Event: He increasingly becomes vindictive and petty toward others
Explanation: His admitted vindictiveness serves his ego at the expense of his relationships.

Event: He values his friendships despite his self-centered nature
Explanation: Appreciating and valuing friendships benefits both him and his friends.

Event: In the finale, he distances himself from the plan to sabotage/launch Pied Piper
Explanation: He tries to shield himself morally/legally by stepping away, offering little help to others facing the dilemma.

Event: When sabotage is detected, he goes to the supernode and implements the sabotage code just in time
Explanation: He risks his own position to decisively prevent a catastrophic global outcome, massively helping others and ultimately himself.

Event: He ultimately sabotages Pied Piper before it can threaten global security
Explanation: Destroying the dangerous system saves the world and later enables his own success.

Event: He starts his own cybersecurity company with Gilfoyle after Pied Piper’s fallout
Explanation: Founding a successful security company is highly beneficial to him and provides valuable protection to others.

Event: He sneaks into the garage to set up one of Gilfoyle's homemade servers
Explanation: His unauthorized meddling gives him no lasting gain and sets up serious damage to others’ equipment.

Event: He flips a single switch on the server setup, breaking $50,000 of equipment and blacking out the neighborhood
Explanation: His impulsive action causes massive financial loss and a blackout with no benefit to himself.

Event: He uses the algorithm to develop a video chat app for his personal use
Explanation: Creating a useful app serves his needs and leads to a broadly well-received product.

Event: The team decides to pivot Pied Piper to his video chat app
Explanation: The pivot raises his status and prospects while giving the company a viable new product.

Event: When Richard leaves, he accepts appointment as CEO of Pied Piper
Explanation: Becoming CEO advances his career and (initially) offers leadership continuity for the company.

Event: He purposely omits a terms of service from the chat app
Explanation: The omission exposes him to massive personal liability and regulatory harm to users/company without any real benefit.

Event: He finds out the app violates COPPA and could incur $21B in fines
Explanation: His earlier negligence now threatens him financially while regulators/users may gain some redress or protection.

Event: He briefly feels relief when told the worthless corporation may shield him
Explanation: Understanding potential corporate protection would modestly benefit him without harming others.

Event: He learns he could be personally liable for $21B because of the missing terms of service
Explanation: His earlier choice now causes extreme self-harm risk while regulators/affected parties stand to benefit from accountability.

Event: Gilfoyle pops a bottle of champagne to celebrate Dinesh's mistake
Explanation: Gilfoyle gains amusement and schadenfreude at Dinesh’s expense, offering no help to Dinesh.

Event: Dinesh and Gilfoyle open their own cybersecurity company together in the distant finale
Explanation: Launching a joint firm greatly benefits both founders and provides strong security services to others.
